title: Sh. Om Prakash vs Resident Commissioner on 11 February, 2008
body: Sh. Om Prakash vs Resident Commissioner on 11 February, 2008 1 IN THE COURT OF Sh. SANJEEV AGGARWAL ADLL. SR. CIVIL JUDGE : DELHI. MCA 139/2006 Sh. Om Prakash S/o Late Sh. Bhagwan Das R/o Quarter No. 33 of Kapoorthala Plot Copernicus Marg, New Delhi. ...APPELLANT V e r s u s 1. Resident Commissioner Kerala House 3, Jantar Mantar Road, New Delhi. 2. The Chief Secretary Govt. of Kerala Thrivananthapuram Kerala. ...RESPONDENTS Date of institution:12.09.2006 Date of judgment: 11.02.2008 J U D G M E N T 1 By this judgment, I will dispose off, an appeal preferred by the appellant, against the impugned order dated 01.09.2006 passed by ld. Civil Judge, Delhi whereby the application of the appellant U/O 39 Rules 1 and 2 CPC was dismissed by the ld. trial court( hereinafter the parties are being referred to by their respective status before the trial court, for the sake of convenience). 2 2 The said impugned order has been assailed on various main grounds inter­alia on the following main grounds :­ a) That the ld. trial court while passing the impugned order had failed to appreciate that no reasonable opportunity to be heard, was granted to the appellant by the respondents as no enquiry was done by the respondents before sending the notice dated 10.02.2006 and also, as per notice dated 26.04.2006, alleged internal enquiry was done by the respondents but the copy of the same was never received by the appellant; b) That the ld. trial court had failed to appreciate, that the procedure of Rule 19 of the G.O.(MS)69/75/PW dated 21.03.1975 was not followed by the respondents; c) That the ld. trial court had failed to consider, that as per Rules 19,20,22 of the G.O.(MS)69/75/PW dated 21.03.1975, if the occupant/allottee commits, violation of any of the rules under the G.O.(MS)69/75/PW, then only the Collector is the competent person to issue notice but in the present case, both the notices dated 10.02.2006 & 26.04.2006 were sent to the appellant by the Resident Commissioner, who was not the competent person to issue notice under the G.O.(MS)69/75/PW. Hence, it is stated that the impugned order deserves to be set aside. 3 3. I have heard ld. counsel for parties and have also gone through the record. The trial court record reveals that the plaintiff had filed a suit on the allegations, that he was working as peon with the respondents w.e.f. 20.12.1982 and was posted at Kerala House, New Delhi and he was alloted a quarter No. 33 of Kapoorthala Plot vide letter dated 17.10.2000 by virtue of his posting in Kerala House, Delhi and that he had an absolutely blotless career and that the defendant No. 1 had issued him a notice dated 10.02.2006 regarding his misconduct against Smt. C. Vijaylakshmi, C.A. to ARC and the plaintiff was directed to vacate the said quarter No. 33 of Kapoorthala Plot, New Delhi and therefore, on 14.02.2006, the plaintiff had requested to supply the complaint of said Smt. C. Vijaylakshmi but till date, the same was not provided to him and thereafter, the plaintiff had filed reply dated 17.02.2006 to the said notice dated 10.02.2006. It was further stated that the defendants had issued another notice dated 26.04.2006 to the plaintiff for vacation of the staff quarter in question. 4. Defence of the defendants before the ld. trial court was that one Smt. C. Vijayalakshmi, C.A. to the Resident Commissioner, Kerala House, New Delhi had submitted a complaint on 31.01.2006 against the misconduct of the plaintiff and his wife, submitting that on 30.01.2006 the plaintiff and his 4 wife had entered into the office of the Addl. Resident Commissioner and had started shouting and abusing the complainant in the filthiest language and had also threatened her of dire consequences. It was further stated that though the plaintiff's wife was not an employee of the office, she had unauthorisedly entered the office and caused obstruction to the official duty of an government employee. 5. It was further stated that on 12.04.2006, the plaintiff's wife had repeated the same kind of action and consequently, the plaintiff and his wife's conduct amounted to, misconduct as per Rule 3 of the Government Servants Conduct Rules and as per para 24(3) of G.O.M. S.69 / 75 /PW dated 21.03.1975. Hence, the plaintiff was in unauthorised occupation of the flat once he was asked by notice 26.04.2006 to vacate the quarter in question. 6. Thereafter, vide detailed order dated 01.09.2006 passed by the ld. Civil Judge, the application of the plaintiff U/O 39 Rules 1 and 2 CPC was dismissed. 7. Vide order dated 11.10.2006, since both the respondents had not appeared before the ld. Predecessor, consequently, both were proceeded exparte and the application of the appellant U/O 41 Rule 5 CPC was allowed and the respondents were restrained to dispossess the appellant from the 5 suit premises till final disposal of appeal. 8. The trial court record, reveals that defendants No. 1 and 2 had filed a number of documents on record, including a complaint dated 31.01.2006 lodged by one Smt. C. Vijayalakshmi, C.A. to RC and another complaint dated 17.04.2006 lodged by her against the same person. Admittedly, the plaintiff had also filed a reply dated 17.02.2006 to the said notice, which was received in the office of the Resident Commissioner and thereafter, a notice for vacation of premises was issued to him vide notice dated 26.04.2006, asking him to vacate the staff quarter in question and in the said notice in para 1 it was stated that an internal enquiry had also been done. 9. It is an admitted case of the parties that the plaintiff was allotted a servant quarter according to the Rules of G.O.M. S.69 / 75 /PW dated 21.03.1975. Therefore, the contention of counsel for appellant, that Public Premises Act is applicable in the present case, is not sustainable, as once the appellant had accepted the allotment of a staff quarter, under the said Rules and he had occupied the same under the aforesaid Rules, thereafter, he cannot be allowed to deny the said rules and to say that Public Premises Act is applicable to his case, as he is estopped from doing so and Rule 24(3) 6 of the G.O.M. S.69 / 75 /PW dated 21.03.1975 provides, that an occupant of a govt. accommodation shall conduct himself decently without causing any inconvenience to the neighbours. It further provides that unnecessary quarrels, state of drunkenness or other undesirable behaviour shall be treated as misconduct and such occupants shall be liable to be evicted on such ground alone without any notice. 10. Ld. counsel for appellant has argued that the word Allottee/occupant means only, the government servant occupying the said flat and since the alleged misconduct, is of the wife of the plaintiff and not of the plaintiff, consequently, the plaintiff/appellant could not have been asked to vacate the premises. This argument of the counsel for appellant is not sustainable as the word allotee/occupant would also mean the family members of the allotee/occupant, who would be staying with him, because if any other interpretation is taken then, no person other than the allotee would be allowed to occupy the quarter in question and it is not the argument of the ld. counsel for appellant that only the appellant was allowed to occupy the quarter in question in his individual capacity as all the government flats are occupied by all the family members ordinarily residing with him. 7 11. Regarding the next contention of the appellant that notice can only be issued by the District Collector under afore rules and not by the Resident Commissioner. This contention is also without any substance as the Resident Commissioner is generally a very senior administrative officer and is senior to the District Collector, who is generally much junior in hierarchy of the administrative machinery, a higher officer can always issue a notice, which could have been issued by his subordinate and in the present case, the Resident Commissioner. 12. Regarding the next contention of the appellant that no rules of natural justice were followed in the present case, therefore, the impugned order was not sustainable. In the present case, the trial court record reveals that the plaintiff had filed a reply dated 17.02.2006, to the notice dated 10.02.2006 issued against him by the office of the Resident Commissioner and which reply was not found satisfactory after an internal enquiry and therefore, the plaintiff/appellant was asked to vacate the quarter in question, therefore, it cannot be said that the plaintiff was not heard as he was given an opportunity to give his explanation to the complaint lodged by one Smt. C. Vijayalakshmi and after internal enquiry, vide notice dated 26.04.2006, his reply was found to be unsatisfactory, consequently, he was asked to vacate 8 the quarter in question. I am satisfied that in the present case, the principles of natural justice have been duly followed. 13. Admittedly, the plaintiff is an employee of the Kerala government, therefore, as discussed above, the provisions of the Public Premises Act are not applicable. Since the allegations against the plaintiff were found to be proved after an internal enquiry vide notice dated 26.04.2006 on account of proved misconduct, and further, the appellant was alloted the quarter in question only as a licensee against certain conditions, stipulated under G.O.M. S.69 / 75 /PW, and since the license was granted subject to afore rules mentioned above, therefore, any breach of those rules by any allottee including the present appellant/plaintiff namely, misconduct in this case, which was found to be proved by an internal enquiry. Therefore, the plaintiff/appellant has failed to make out any prima facie case in his favour for further occupation of the quarter in question and after the notice of vacation being served upon him, he becomes an unauthorised occupant and as per settled law no injunction can be granted in favour of the appellant in these circumstances. Secondly, even otherwise, the appellant has not challenged, the order dated 10.02.2006 and the notice dated 26.04.2006 issued by the office of the Resident Commissioner, Kerala as by seeking 9 relief of declaration, as he has not sought any relief of declaration for declaring those notices null and void. 14. Consequently, looking from every angle, the appellant has failed to make out any prima facie case in his favour, the balance of convenience also does not lie in his favour nor he shall suffer any irreparable loss and injury if he is evicted from the quarter in question. 15. In view of the afore discussion, I am of the view that the ld. trial court, had rightly dismissed the application of the plaintiff/appellant U/O 39 Rules 1 and 2 CPC and the impugned order dated 01.09.2006 of the ld. trial court, consequently suffers from no infirmity or illegality. As a resultant, appeal is dismissed and the interim order dated 11.10.2006 is vacated. However, in the interest of justice, the respondents are restrained from dispossessing the appellant from the quarter No. 33 of Kapoorthala Plot, Copernicus Marg, New Delhi, till 11.03.2008, in order to enable the appellant to prefer a further appeal, if so advised. Trial court record be sent back alongwith a copy of this order and appeal file be consigned to Record Room. Announced in the open court on 11.02.2008. (Sanjeev Aggarwal) Addl. Senior Civil Judge : Delhi. one spare copy attached.
